refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a performance target and (2) sustaining it over time. The refined version separates these concerns while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing how availability is achieved (e.g., redundancy, predictive maintenance, rapid repair) and focuses on the measurable outcome in the problem domain. The phrase 'achieve and maintain' captures both the initial attainment and sustained performance without introducing implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, scheduled vs. unscheduled maintenance events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Downtime) / Total Time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that the measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold for the full evaluation window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall achieve the customer-defined operational availability target at the start of the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall sustain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target throughout the agreed evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant critical subsystems (power, control, sensing) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity.
DP2: Design modular architecture with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces to enable rapid replacement of failed units without system shutdown.
DP3: Establish predictive maintenance framework using condition monitoring and degradation models to detect and address failures before they impact availability.
DP4: Implement graceful degradation strategy where non-critical functions are suspended to preserve core operational capability during partial failures.
DP5: Deploy distributed control architecture with local autonomy and asynchronous coordination to reduce single-point control failures and communication bottlenecks.
DP6: Establish comprehensive fault detection and isolation (FDI) system with automated diagnostics and operator alerting to minimize mean-time-to-repair (MTTR).
DP7: Implement configuration management and rapid reconfiguration capability to restore service by switching to alternate operational modes or resource allocations.
DP8: Design for testability and continuous health monitoring with built-in self-test (BIST) and real-time telemetry to validate system state and predict failures.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP_Composite: Achieve availability ≥0.97 through integrated strategy combining: (1) redundancy of critical subsystems with automatic failover (DP1), (2) fault detection and isolation with minimized MTTR (DP6), (3) predictive maintenance framework to reduce failure frequency (DP3), and (4) graceful degradation to preserve core function during partial failures (DP4). These DPs operate synergistically: redundancy masks failures, FDI enables rapid recovery, predictive maintenance reduces failure occurrence, and graceful degradation maintains partial service. Selection prioritizes logical independence of failure modes and recovery mechanisms over technology-specific implementation.

potential_sources: DP1 (redundancy) and DP6 (FDI) are tightly coupled: FDI must detect which redundant path has failed to trigger correct failover. Mitigation: define clear FDI-to-failover interface contract.
DP3 (predictive maintenance) and DP6 (FDI) share sensor/telemetry infrastructure. Mitigation: design unified health monitoring data model to avoid duplication.

DP4 (graceful degradation) and DP1 (redundancy) may conflict if degradation mode consumes redundant capacity. Mitigation: establish priority rules and capacity planning to ensure redundancy is preserved.

DP1 and DP2 (modularity) are complementary but require coordinated design: redundant subsystems must be packaged as independent, swappable modules. Mitigation: enforce module boundary discipline in IM layer.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is acceptable because dependencies are logical and well-defined. The composite DP avoids the anti-pattern of relying on a single mechanism (e.g., redundancy alone without FDI leads to undetected failures; FDI alone without redundancy cannot mask failures). Cross-domain dependencies (e.g., FDI data feeding predictive models) are intentional and support the availability goal.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Redundant subsystems can be designed to fail independently (no common-mode failures). RISK: Shared environmental stressors (temperature, vibration, power quality) may cause correlated failures. Mitigation: thermal/mechanical isolation, independent power conditioning, diversity in component selection.

ASSUMPTION: Fault detection latency is negligible compared to MTTR. RISK: If FDI detection delay is significant, availability gains from fast recovery are eroded. Mitigation: define maximum acceptable detection latency and validate in design.

ASSUMPTION: Predictive maintenance models are sufficiently accurate to reduce unplanned failures. RISK: Model degradation over time, environmental drift, or novel failure modes not in training data. Mitigation: continuous model validation, fallback to reactive maintenance, operator override capability.

ASSUMPTION: Graceful degradation does not violate safety or regulatory constraints. RISK: Reduced functionality may breach worksite safety plan or operational policies. Mitigation: explicit safety analysis of degraded modes; coordinate with safety plan.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is long enough to observe failure statistics and validate 0.97 target. RISK: Short evaluation window may not capture rare failure modes or seasonal variations. Mitigation: define minimum evaluation duration; use accelerated testing or historical data to supplement.

ASSUMPTION: Operator training and procedures support rapid diagnosis and recovery. RISK: Human factors (fatigue, error, unfamiliarity) may increase MTTR. Mitigation: design for operator support (clear diagnostics, decision aids); include training in acceptance criteria.

ASSUMPTION: System boundaries and interfaces are stable during evaluation period. RISK: Changes to external systems (power supply, network, worksite conditions) may degrade availability. Mitigation: define interface contracts and change control process.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold (≥0.97 measured over the agreed evaluation period) by implementing a composite availability strategy that: (1) masks single-point failures through redundancy of critical subsystems with automatic failover, (2) minimizes mean-time-to-repair through rapid fault detection and isolation, (3) reduces failure frequency through predictive maintenance, and (4) preserves core operational capability during partial failures through graceful degradation. The solution shall operate within the worksite safety plan and operational policies, with explicit validation that degraded modes do not violate safety constraints. Success is measured by: (a) measured availability ≥0.97 over the evaluation period, (b) mean-time-between-failures (MTBF) and mean-time-to-repair (MTTR) targets derived from the availability equation, (c) zero safety-critical failures, and (d) operator acceptance of diagnostic and recovery procedures.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Integrated availability and resilience management module responsible for orchestrating redundancy, fault detection/isolation, predictive maintenance coordination, and graceful degradation across the NGHE Solution ecosystem.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound health, performance, and diagnostic data from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to feed fault detection, predictive maintenance models, and degradation state assessment.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound alerts and directives for failover activation, graceful degradation mode transitions, and maintenance scheduling to Vehicle and Teleoperation.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface to negotiate task constraints during degraded modes and to receive operational context for predictive maintenance prioritization.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Redundant communication paths for failover signaling, telemetry aggregation, and coordination with distributed redundancy nodes.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Safety Services is the natural owner of availability orchestration, fault response, and resilience logic. Redundancy failover (DP1), FDI (DP6), and graceful degradation (DP4) are core safety-critical functions. Predictive maintenance coordination (DP3) fits as a proactive safety measure. Minor diffusion: Vehicle and Teleoperation must implement redundant hardware and graceful degradation logic locally, but Safety Services owns the coordination and decision layer.

Poor fit as primary boundary. Vehicle is a physical subsystem; it can host redundant actuators and sensors (DP1 hardware), but cannot own the cross-system failover orchestration, teleoperation fault detection, or predictive maintenance for the entire solution. Availability strategy would diffuse across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution, violating cohesion.

Poor fit as primary boundary. Teleoperation handles remote control and situational awareness but lacks authority over Vehicle redundancy, network-level failover, or system-wide predictive maintenance. Availability logic would scatter across multiple modules.

Acceptable but overly broad. NGHE Solution is the top-level system container and could theoretically own availability strategy, but it is too coarse-grained. Safety Services is a more specific, cohesive boundary within NGHE Solution that isolates resilience logic from mission planning, navigation, and task execution. Choosing NGHE Solution would blur ownership and reduce architectural clarity.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the most specific and cohesive module boundary for the composite availability DP. It owns the decision logic for redundancy activation, fault detection/isolation response, predictive maintenance scheduling, and graceful degradation mode transitions. Vehicle and Teleoperation implement the physical/functional mechanisms (redundant hardware, sensor fusion, mode-aware control), but Safety Services orchestrates their interaction to achieve the 0.97 availability target. This separation preserves logical independence of failure modes and recovery mechanisms while avoiding technology-specific implementation details.
containment_risks: Risk: Predictive maintenance (DP3) may require deep domain knowledge of Vehicle subsystem wear patterns and Teleoperation communication reliability. If Safety Services lacks this knowledge, maintenance predictions could be inaccurate. Mitigation: Safety Services consumes Telemetry and domain-specific health models from Vehicle and Teleoperation; it does not implement the models itself.

Risk: Graceful degradation (DP4) requires Vehicle and Teleoperation to support multiple operational modes (full, reduced, minimal). If these subsystems do not expose mode-switching interfaces, Safety Services cannot orchestrate degradation. Mitigation: Establish explicit mode-control interfaces (via SafetyAlert) as part of the DP realization contract.

Risk: Failover latency depends on Network Link reliability and Vehicle/Teleoperation response time. Safety Services owns the decision but not the execution speed. Mitigation: Define SLAs for failover response time and validate that Vehicle and Teleoperation can meet them.

Risk: Redundancy (DP1) may require hardware duplication in Vehicle (e.g., dual motors, dual sensors). Safety Services cannot own this; it only orchestrates activation. Mitigation: Ensure Vehicle design includes redundant subsystems and exposes their status via Telemetry.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability ≥0.97 is measurable over the agreed evaluation period via telemetry aggregation and incident logging. Safety Services receives Telemetry inbound and issues SafetyAlert directives, creating an observable control loop. Verification requires: (1) baseline failure rate and MTTR data from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems, (2) failover activation logs and response times, (3) predictive maintenance accuracy metrics, and (4) graceful degradation mode transition logs. All are traceable to Safety Services orchestration decisions.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Safety Services owns the availability orchestration logic (DP_Composite decision layer); Vehicle and Teleoperation own the physical/functional mechanisms (redundant hardware, mode-aware control, sensor fusion). The FR is allocated to Safety Services as the primary owner, with dependencies on Vehicle and Teleoperation for mechanism implementation. Interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, Network Link) establish the contract boundaries.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP_Composite logically maps to FR. The four sub-DPs (redundancy, FDI, predictive maintenance, graceful degradation) are necessary and sufficient to achieve availability ≥0.97. The synergistic interaction is well-articulated: redundancy masks failures, FDI enables rapid recovery (reducing MTTR), predictive maintenance reduces failure frequency (reducing λ), and graceful degradation preserves partial service during transients. The DP prioritizes logical independence of failure modes and recovery mechanisms over technology specifics, which is appropriate for a functional requirement. No independence axiom violation detected.

diffusion_risks: DP3 (Predictive Maintenance): Safety Services owns scheduling and coordination, but Vehicle and Teleoperation must provide domain-specific health models and wear-pattern data. If health model ownership is ambiguous (e.g., both Safety Services and Vehicle claim responsibility for motor wear prediction), diffusion occurs. Mitigation: Establish explicit contract—Vehicle provides raw telemetry and domain model; Safety Services applies predictive logic and schedules maintenance.

DP4 (Graceful Degradation): Safety Services owns mode-transition decisions, but Vehicle and Teleoperation must implement mode-aware control logic. If mode definitions or switching logic scatter across modules (e.g., Vehicle implements partial thrust reduction independently of Safety Services directives), coherence breaks. Mitigation: Define mode state machine in Safety Services; Vehicle and Teleoperation implement mode handlers as passive responders to SafetyAlert directives.

DP1 (Redundancy Failover): Safety Services owns failover orchestration, but Vehicle and Teleoperation own redundant hardware and local failover detection. If failover decisions are made at multiple levels (e.g., Vehicle autonomously switches to backup motor without Safety Services knowledge), coordination fails. Mitigation: Establish clear authority: Safety Services makes failover decisions; Vehicle/Teleoperation execute and report status via Telemetry.

DP6 (Fault Detection/Isolation): Safety Services owns the FDI decision logic, but Vehicle and Teleoperation must provide sensor fusion and diagnostic data. If FDI algorithms are duplicated across modules (e.g., Vehicle implements local fault detection independently of Safety Services), MTTR optimization is compromised. Mitigation: Centralize FDI logic in Safety Services; Vehicle and Teleoperation provide raw sensor data and local health indicators via Telemetry.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services containment score of 0.92 is justified. The module is the natural owner of availability orchestration, fault response, and resilience decision logic. Minor diffusion (0.08) is acceptable and well-mitigated: Vehicle and Teleoperation implement redundant mechanisms locally, but Safety Services owns the coordination and decision layer. The four identified containment risks are appropriately flagged and mitigations are sound. Recommend formalizing the DP<->IM contract via explicit interface specifications: (1) Telemetry schema must include health indicators, failure codes, and mode status; (2) SafetyAlert schema must include failover directives, mode-transition commands, and maintenance scheduling; (3) Vehicle and Teleoperation must expose mode-control and redundancy-status interfaces; (4) Network Link must guarantee failover signaling latency SLA (e.g., <100ms for critical failovers).

recommended_revisions: Formalize DP3 (Predictive Maintenance) ownership contract: Safety Services owns scheduling and decision logic; Vehicle and Teleoperation own domain-specific health models and provide telemetry. Document the health model interface (e.g., wear indicators, remaining useful life estimates) in the Telemetry schema.

Formalize DP4 (Graceful Degradation) mode state machine: Define operational modes (full, reduced, minimal) and mode-transition rules in Safety Services. Vehicle and Teleoperation implement mode handlers as passive responders to SafetyAlert mode-transition directives. Document mode definitions and switching latency SLAs.

Formalize DP1 (Redundancy Failover) authority hierarchy: Establish that Safety Services makes failover decisions based on FDI results; Vehicle and Teleoperation execute failover and report status. Define failover response time SLA (e.g., <50ms for critical subsystems) and validate Vehicle/Teleoperation can meet it.

Centralize DP6 (Fault Detection/Isolation) logic in Safety Services: Avoid duplicating FDI algorithms across modules. Vehicle and Teleoperation provide raw sensor data and local health indicators; Safety Services applies FDI logic and issues SafetyAlert directives. Document FDI algorithm assumptions and sensor data requirements.

Define Telemetry schema explicitly: Include health indicators (failure codes, mode status, redundancy status, wear estimates), diagnostic data (sensor readings, communication latency), and timestamps. Ensure all four DPs can consume required telemetry.

Define SafetyAlert schema explicitly: Include failover activation directives, graceful degradation mode-transition commands, and maintenance scheduling recommendations. Ensure Vehicle and Teleoperation can parse and execute all directive types.

Establish Network Link failover signaling SLA: Redundant communication paths must guarantee failover signal delivery within <100ms (or customer-defined threshold). Validate that Network Link design meets this SLA.

Add acceptance criterion for predictive maintenance accuracy: Define acceptable false-positive and false-negative rates for maintenance predictions. Establish baseline from Vehicle and Teleoperation domain expertise.

Add acceptance criterion for graceful degradation mode coverage: Ensure Vehicle and Teleoperation support all defined operational modes and can transition between them within SLA (e.g., <5s for non-critical transitions).

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence, and the logical decomposition is sound. However, the recommended_revisions identify critical formalization gaps at the DP-IM boundary that must be resolved before proceeding to sub-FR derivation. Specifically: (1) DP3 and DP4 ownership contracts are ambiguous (Vehicle/Teleoperation domain model responsibility vs. Safety Services orchestration), (2) DP1 failover authority hierarchy lacks explicit SLA definition, (3) DP6 centralization vs. duplication risk is flagged but not yet contractually enforced, and (4) interface schemas (Telemetry, SafetyAlert) are referenced but not formally specified. Given high safety criticality, these ambiguities must be resolved at the current level before decomposing further. Proceeding to sub-FRs without formalizing these contracts risks diffusion propagation into lower levels, where correction becomes exponentially more costly. A REVISE_CURRENT_LEVEL decision ensures architectural coherence and traceability.

revise.why: The four sub-DPs (Redundancy Failover, FDI, Predictive Maintenance, Graceful Degradation) are logically correct and necessary, but their realization via Safety Services, Vehicle, and Teleoperation modules requires explicit contractual formalization. Revisions needed: (1) Formalize DP3 ownership: Safety Services owns predictive scheduling logic; Vehicle/Teleoperation own domain-specific health models and provide telemetry via standardized schema (wear indicators, RUL estimates, failure codes). (2) Formalize DP4 mode state machine: Define operational modes (full, reduced, minimal) and transition rules in Safety Services; Vehicle/Teleoperation implement passive mode handlers responding to SafetyAlert directives. (3) Formalize DP1 failover authority: Safety Services makes failover decisions; Vehicle/Teleoperation execute and report status within <50ms SLA. (4) Formalize DP6 centralization: FDI logic resides in Safety Services; Vehicle/Teleoperation provide raw sensor data and local health indicators, not duplicate FDI algorithms. (5) Define Telemetry schema: health indicators (failure codes, mode status, redundancy status, wear estimates), diagnostic data (sensor readings, latency), timestamps. (6) Define SafetyAlert schema: failover directives, mode-transition commands, maintenance recommendations. (7) Establish Network Link SLA: failover signaling latency <100ms (or customer-defined threshold). These revisions transform the DP layer from logically sound to architecturally contractual, enabling unambiguous allocation and verification.

assumptions: Safety Services is the natural owner of availability orchestration and can enforce centralized FDI, predictive maintenance scheduling, and mode-transition decisions without performance degradation.
Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems can implement passive mode handlers and provide standardized telemetry without requiring autonomous local failover or FDI logic.
Network Link can guarantee failover signaling latency <100ms under all operational conditions (nominal, degraded, transient).
Predictive maintenance models (wear patterns, RUL estimation) can be formalized as domain-specific contracts provided by Vehicle/Teleoperation and consumed by Safety Services without tight coupling.
Graceful degradation modes (full, reduced, minimal) can be defined statically in Safety Services and do not require dynamic mode discovery or Vehicle/Teleoperation autonomy.
Telemetry aggregation and SafetyAlert directive execution are deterministic and can be verified via logs and telemetry replay.
open_risks: DP3 (Predictive Maintenance) diffusion risk: If Vehicle or Teleoperation independently implement wear prediction or maintenance scheduling (e.g., local motor health monitoring), Safety Services loses visibility and coordination authority. Mitigation: Formalize contract—Vehicle/Teleoperation provide raw health telemetry; Safety Services owns scheduling decisions.

DP4 (Graceful Degradation) diffusion risk: If Vehicle or Teleoperation implement mode-aware control logic independently of Safety Services directives (e.g., Vehicle autonomously reduces thrust when sensors detect overheating), mode coherence breaks. Mitigation: Define mode state machine in Safety Services; Vehicle/Teleoperation implement passive mode handlers.

DP1 (Redundancy Failover) coordination risk: If Vehicle or Teleoperation make autonomous failover decisions without Safety Services knowledge, MTTR optimization and availability orchestration are compromised. Mitigation: Establish clear authority—Safety Services decides; Vehicle/Teleoperation execute and report.

DP6 (Fault Detection/Isolation) duplication risk: If Vehicle or Teleoperation implement local FDI algorithms in parallel with Safety Services FDI, inconsistent fault diagnosis and delayed recovery can occur. Mitigation: Centralize FDI logic in Safety Services; Vehicle/Teleoperation provide sensor data only.

Interface schema ambiguity: Telemetry and SafetyAlert schemas are not yet formally specified. If Vehicle/Teleoperation implement telemetry differently than Safety Services expects, data loss or misinterpretation occurs. Mitigation: Formalize schemas before sub-FR derivation.

Network Link SLA risk: If failover signaling latency exceeds <100ms threshold, Safety Services cannot respond to failures within required MTTR budget. Mitigation: Validate Network Link design and establish redundant communication paths.

Mode transition latency risk: If Vehicle/Teleoperation cannot transition between modes within SLA (e.g., <5s for non-critical), graceful degradation effectiveness is compromised. Mitigation: Add acceptance criterion and validate during IM design.

Predictive maintenance accuracy risk: If health models produce high false-positive or false-negative rates, Safety Services will either over-schedule maintenance (cost) or under-schedule (availability loss). Mitigation: Define acceptable accuracy thresholds and baseline from domain expertise.
evidence_needed: Formalized DP3 ownership contract: Document health model interface (wear indicators, RUL estimates, failure codes) and Safety Services scheduling logic. Include examples of how Vehicle/Teleoperation provide telemetry and Safety Services makes maintenance decisions.

Formalized DP4 mode state machine: Define operational modes (full, reduced, minimal), transition rules, and mode-handler responsibilities. Include state diagram and SafetyAlert directive format for mode transitions.

Formalized DP1 failover authority hierarchy: Document that Safety Services makes failover decisions based on FDI results; Vehicle/Teleoperation execute and report status. Include failover response time SLA (<50ms) and validation plan.

Formalized DP6 centralization contract: Document that FDI logic resides in Safety Services; Vehicle/Teleoperation provide raw sensor data and local health indicators. Include FDI algorithm assumptions and sensor data requirements.

Telemetry schema specification: Define health indicators (failure codes, mode status, redundancy status, wear estimates), diagnostic data (sensor readings, communication latency), and timestamps. Include examples and validation rules.

SafetyAlert schema specification: Define failover activation directives, graceful degradation mode-transition commands, and maintenance scheduling recommendations. Include examples and parsing requirements for Vehicle/Teleoperation.

Network Link SLA validation: Confirm that redundant communication paths guarantee failover signal delivery within <100ms. Include latency measurements under nominal, degraded, and transient conditions.

Predictive maintenance accuracy baseline: Establish acceptable false-positive and false-negative rates from Vehicle/Teleoperation domain expertise. Include historical failure data and wear-pattern analysis.

Graceful degradation mode coverage validation: Confirm that Vehicle/Teleoperation support all defined operational modes and can transition between them within SLA (e.g., <5s for non-critical transitions).
Interface compatibility testing: Validate that Vehicle/Teleoperation can parse and execute all Telemetry and SafetyAlert schemas without ambiguity or data loss.
